subject: Hematology
front: How is MCV (mean corpuscular volume) calculated?

subject: Hematology
front: How is MCH (mean corpuscular hemoglobin) calculated?

subject: Hematology
front: How is MCHC (mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration) calculated?

subject: Hematology
front: How can hematocrit (Hct) be estimated from hemoglobin?

subject: Hematology
front: At what angle is the spreader held when making a peripheral blood smear?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the shape of a well-made peripheral smear and what are its three parts?

subject: Hematology
front: Which class of stain is used for peripheral smears, and give examples.
back: Romanowsky stains - e.g. Leishman, Giemsa, Wright, Jenner.

subject: Hematology
front: In Romanowsky stains, which component is the basic (positively charged) dye and which is the acidic dye?
back: Basic = methylene blue; acidic = eosin Y.

subject: Hematology
front: Where does hematopoiesis occur in the 3rd week of gestation, and which hemoglobins are produced there?
back: Yolk sac; produces Portland Hb and Gower 1 & 2.

subject: Hematology
front: Where does hematopoiesis occur in the 3rd month of gestation, and which hemoglobin predominates?

subject: Hematology
front: What are the hematopoietic sites in the 4th month of gestation?

subject: Hematology
front: Which bones perform hematopoiesis up to 20 years of age?
back: Bone marrow of long AND flat bones.

subject: Hematology
front: Which bones perform hematopoiesis after 20 years of age?
back: Bone marrow of flat bones only.

subject: Hematology
front: What are 'nurse cells' in erythropoiesis?
back: Bone marrow macrophages that assist in RBC development.

subject: Hematology
front: Which transcription factors drive RBC development?

subject: Hematology
front: What surface marker identifies the pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell?

subject: Hematology
front: List the RBC maturation sequence from stem cell to mature red cell.
back: Pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell (CD34+) -> proerythroblast (pronormoblast) -> erythroblast (normoblast) -> reticulocyte -> erythrocyte.

subject: Hematology
front: Which is the first cell in which hemoglobin can be visualised on electron microscopy?

subject: Hematology
front: List the erythroblast (normoblast) substages in order.
back: Basophilic erythroblast -> polychromatophilic erythroblast -> orthochromatic erythroblast.

subject: Hematology
front: At which stage does hemoglobin synthesis begin?

subject: Hematology
front: Which is the first cell in which hemoglobin is visible on light microscopy?

subject: Hematology
front: At which step is the nucleus (and some organelles) lost?
back: When the orthochromatic erythroblast becomes a reticulocyte (in the marrow).

subject: Hematology
front: When are the remaining organelles lost?
back: When the reticulocyte becomes an erythrocyte (in the blood).

subject: Hematology
front: How long does a reticulocyte typically spend in marrow versus blood?
back: About 3 days in marrow and 1 day in blood.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the lifespan of an erythrocyte?

subject: Hematology
front: What fraction of circulating RBCs are reticulocytes?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the earliest committed cell in erythropoiesis?

subject: Hematology
front: Which erythroid cell has the highest number of EPO receptors?

subject: Hematology
front: What effect do EPO, GH, T3, cortisol and testosterone have on RBC production?

subject: Hematology
front: What effect does estrogen have on RBC production?

subject: Hematology
front: What is a reticulocyte in relation to RBCs?

subject: Hematology
front: Reticulocyte count is used to monitor which therapy, and when does it rise?
back: Iron therapy; it rises in 5-7 days.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the youngest RBC stage normally seen in peripheral blood?

subject: Hematology
front: What type of stain demonstrates reticulocytes and what does it stain?
back: Supravital stain; it stains the RNA of the live cell.

subject: Hematology
front: Name two supravital stains used for reticulocytes.
back: Brilliant cresyl blue and new methylene blue.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the formula for the corrected reticulocyte count.
back: Corrected retic count = Retic% x (Patient Hb / Normal Hb).

subject: Hematology
front: Give the formula for the reticulocyte production index (RPI).
back: RPI = Corrected retic count / Maturation time.

subject: Hematology
front: In RPI, list the maturation (correction) times for PCV values of 45, 35, 25 and 15.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the two bone marrow aspiration needles described.
back: Salah's needle (with a side flange) and Klima's needle.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the core bone marrow biopsy needle called, and its older name?
back: Core BM biopsy needle; the old name is Jamshidi needle.

subject: Hematology
front: Preferred site for BMA/BMB in an adult?

subject: Hematology
front: Preferred site for BMA/BMB in a child?

subject: Hematology
front: Is a low platelet count a contraindication to BMA/BMB?
back: No; if platelets < 20,000, transfuse first and then proceed.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the MCV cut-offs for microcytic, normocytic and macrocytic anemia.
back: Microcytic MCV < 80 fL; normocytic MCV 80-100 fL; macrocytic MCV > 100 fL.

subject: Hematology
front: Hemolytic anemia falls in which MCV category, and what is its reticulocyte index?

subject: Hematology
front: Which two serum markers are raised in hemolytic anemia?
back: Increased serum LDH and increased serum bilirubin.

subject: Hematology
front: Name two normocytic, non-hemolytic anemias.
back: Early IDA and anemia of chronic disease (ACD).

subject: Hematology
front: Hemolytic anemias are divided into which two broad categories?

subject: Hematology
front: Intrinsic hemolytic anemias due to Hb defects - name them.

subject: Hematology
front: Intrinsic hemolytic anemias due to membrane defects - name them.

subject: Hematology
front: Which membrane defect is the only acquired cause of intrinsic hemolysis?

subject: Hematology
front: Intrinsic hemolytic anemias due to enzyme defects - name them.
back: G6PD deficiency, pyruvate kinase (PK) deficiency, hexokinase deficiency.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the extrinsic causes of hemolytic anemia.

subject: Hematology
front: MAHA is associated with which three conditions?

subject: Hematology
front: What are the causes/categories of megaloblastic anemia?
back: B12 deficiency, B9 (folate) deficiency, B1 deficiency, orotic aciduria, and certain drugs.

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of B12 deficiency.

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of B9 (folate) deficiency.
back: Junk foods (poor diet), jejunal resection, celiac sprue.

subject: Hematology
front: Which drugs cause megaloblastic anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the bone marrow appearance in megaloblastic anemia?
back: Large erythroid precursors with sieve-like, immature chromatin (megaloblasts).

subject: Hematology
front: Megaloblastic anemia may cause pancytopenia; what M:E change is seen in the marrow?

subject: Hematology
front: What type of hypersensitivity is pernicious anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the type 1 (blocking) antibody in pernicious anemia.
back: It blocks binding of B12 to intrinsic factor (IF).

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the type 2 (binding) antibody in pernicious anemia.
back: It blocks binding of the B12-IF complex to cubilin (its receptor).

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the type 3 antibody in pernicious anemia.

subject: Hematology
front: Gross finding of the stomach in pernicious anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: Light-microscopy finding in the gastric mucosa in pernicious anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: By which routes is B12 given, and which route is avoided?
back: IM or deep SC; never IV.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the Schilling test used for?
back: To determine the cause of B12 deficiency by measuring urinary excretion of radioactive B12.

subject: Hematology
front: Schilling Stage 1: setup and interpretation.
back: Radioactive oral B12 + unlabeled IM B12; high urinary *B12 = dietary B12 deficiency, low = proceed to stage 2.

subject: Hematology
front: Schilling Stage 2: setup and interpretation.
back: Radioactive oral B12 + oral intrinsic factor; high *B12 = pernicious anemia, low = proceed to stage 3.

subject: Hematology
front: Schilling Stage 3: setup and interpretation.
back: Radioactive oral B12 + oral antibiotics; high *B12 = small intestinal bacterial overgrowth, low = proceed to stage 4.

subject: Hematology
front: Schilling Stage 4: setup and interpretation.
back: Radioactive oral B12 + pancreatic enzymes; high *B12 = pancreatic insufficiency, low = other causes (e.g. ileal resection).

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of nonmegaloblastic macrocytic anemia.
back: Liver failure, chronic alcoholism, hypothyroidism, cytotoxic drugs, and hemolysis (reticulocytosis raising MCV).

subject: Hematology
front: Why can hemolysis cause macrocytosis?
back: Reticulocytosis raises the MCV because reticulocytes are larger than mature RBCs.

subject: Hematology
front: Lab/urine features of intravascular hemolysis (IVH).

subject: Hematology
front: Conditions causing predominantly intravascular hemolysis.
back: PNH, MAHA, macroangiopathic AHA, malaria, cold hemolysin.

subject: Hematology
front: Clinical features of extravascular hemolysis (EVH).

subject: Hematology
front: Conditions causing predominantly extravascular hemolysis.
back: HS, HE, SCA, thalassemia, cold agglutinin.

subject: Hematology
front: Which conditions cause BOTH intra- and extravascular hemolysis?

subject: Hematology
front: Hapten model of drug-induced hemolytic anemia: example drug and mechanism.
back: Penicillin; IgG against the drug bound to the RBC causes hemolysis by complement/phagocytosis (an antigenic-drug mechanism).

subject: Hematology
front: Immune-complex model of drug-induced hemolytic anemia: example drug.
back: Quinidine; drug-carrier-antibody-C3b complex binds C3b receptors -> hemolysis by complement (antigenic-drug mechanism).

subject: Hematology
front: Autoimmune model of drug-induced hemolytic anemia: example drug and how it differs.
back: Alpha-methyldopa; antibody targets normal RBC antigens -> hemolysis by phagocytosis (a tolerance-breaking drug).

subject: Hematology
front: How are the drug-induced hemolytic anemia mechanisms grouped?
back: Hapten and immune-complex models = antigenic drugs; autoimmune model = tolerance-breaking drug.

subject: Hematology
front: Elliptocyte: which membrane protein is defective, and which disease results?
back: Spectrin defect; leads to hereditary elliptocytosis (HE).

subject: Hematology
front: What surface-area change defines a spherocyte?

subject: Hematology
front: Which membrane protein defect underlies the spherocyte?

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of spherocytes.
back: AIHA (most common), HS, G6PD, burns, blood-transfusion reaction.

subject: Hematology
front: Mushroom / pincer cells indicate a defect in which protein?

subject: Hematology
front: Which is the most abundant RBC membrane protein?

subject: Hematology
front: Which RBC membrane protein is NOT defective in HS?

subject: Hematology
front: Which protein maintains the biconcave shape of the RBC?

subject: Hematology
front: A defect in which RBC membrane protein is the most severe?

subject: Hematology
front: In HS, give the order of defect frequency and what causes the autosomal-recessive form.
back: Ankyrin > Band 3; the autosomal-recessive form is due to a spectrin defect. (FLAG: the exact % in the source is hard to read - appears ~31-34%.)

subject: Hematology
front: What is the inheritance pattern of HS?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the RBC lifespan in HS?

subject: Hematology
front: List the diagnostic lab findings in HS.
back: Increased osmotic fragility, increased MCHC, increased RDW, normal MCV, normal MCH.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the confirmatory test for HS?

subject: Hematology
front: What causes an aplastic crisis in HS?

subject: Hematology
front: What triggers a haemolytic crisis in HS?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the management of HS?

subject: Hematology
front: Which RBC inclusions are grouped under the supravital-stain heading in these notes?
back: Heinz bodies, Howell-Jolly bodies, Pappenheimer bodies, Cabot rings.

subject: Hematology
front: Heinz bodies: composition and associated condition?
back: Composition = denatured Hb; condition = G6PD deficiency.

subject: Hematology
front: Howell-Jolly bodies: composition (as written in the notes)?
back: RNA remnant. (FLAG: notes say 'RNA remnant'; standard teaching is a nuclear/DNA remnant.)

subject: Hematology
front: Howell-Jolly bodies: associated conditions?

subject: Hematology
front: Pappenheimer bodies: composition and associated condition?
back: Composition = iron deposits; condition = sideroblastic anemia.

subject: Hematology
front: Cabot rings: composition and associated conditions?
back: Composition = microtubules; conditions = thalassemia and megaloblastic anemia.

subject: Hematology
front: Fine basophilic stippling is associated with which deficiency?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the significance of a macro-ovalocyte?
back: It is the earliest finding of B12 deficiency.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the criteria for a hypersegmented neutrophil.
back: Greater than or equal to 5% of neutrophils with 5 lobes, OR at least 1 neutrophil with 6 lobes.

subject: Hematology
front: Bite cells (degmacytes) indicate which enzyme defect?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the inheritance of G6PD deficiency?

subject: Hematology
front: Which ethnic descent is associated with G6PD deficiency, and which form is most severe?
back: African/Mediterranean descent; the Mediterranean variant is the most severe form.

subject: Hematology
front: What type(s) of hemolysis occur in G6PD deficiency?
back: Both intravascular and extravascular hemolysis (IVH + EVH).

subject: Hematology
front: What malaria-related advantage does G6PD deficiency confer?

subject: Hematology
front: Name the diagnostic tests for G6PD deficiency.
back: G6PD enzyme assay and the methemoglobin (MetHb) reduction test.

subject: Hematology
front: Why do bite cells in G6PD deficiency cause no HSM or gallstones, and which cells are affected?
back: Hemolysis is transient (so no HSM/gallstones); it affects the older RBCs.

subject: Hematology
front: List the non-drug triggers of oxidative hemolysis in G6PD deficiency.
back: Chronic infections (e.g. pneumonia) and fava beans.

subject: Hematology
front: List the drugs that precipitate hemolysis in G6PD deficiency.
back: Menadione, primaquine/tafenoquine (> chloroquine), nalidixic acid, nitrofurantoin, isoniazid, sulfonamide, dapsone.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the basic mechanism of sideroblastic anemia?
back: Excess iron due to defective heme synthesis.

subject: Hematology
front: Which enzyme is deficient in the X-linked recessive form of sideroblastic anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: How do isoniazid and B6 deficiency cause sideroblastic anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: Which enzymes does lead poisoning inhibit?

subject: Hematology
front: How does alcohol cause sideroblastic anemia?
back: It acts as a mitochondrial toxin.

subject: Hematology
front: What type of basophilic stippling is seen in sideroblastic anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: Define a ringed sideroblast and the stain used.
back: Perl's/Prussian blue stain; at least 5 iron perinuclear granules covering one-third of the nucleus.

subject: Hematology
front: What is an important differential diagnosis of sideroblastic anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: Drepanocytes are also called what?

subject: Hematology
front: Give the order of disease severity by hemoglobin combination.

subject: Hematology
front: List the manifestations of a vaso-occlusive crisis.
back: Stroke, acute chest syndrome, dactylitis, priapism, fish-mouth vertebrae.

subject: Hematology
front: What causes an aplastic crisis in SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: What triggers a haemolytic crisis in SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: Name the four crises of sickle cell anemia.
back: Vaso-occlusive, aplastic, haemolytic, and sequestration crisis.

subject: Hematology
front: Outline the management of acute chest syndrome.
back: Supplemental O2, macrolides + cephalosporins, blood transfusion, incentive spirometry.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the inheritance pattern of SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: Which ethnic descent is associated with SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: What happens to the ESR and the spleen in SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the properties of the sickle-cell polymer.

subject: Hematology
front: Which factors increase sickling, and what index do they raise?
back: Acidosis, hypoxia, and dehydration; they increase MCHC.

subject: Hematology
front: Which factor decreases sickling, and what is the clinical consequence?
back: HbF; clinical features manifest only after 6 months of age.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the screening test for SCA and how is it performed?
back: Sickling test - artificial hypoxia using 2% Na metabisulphite.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for SCA?

subject: Hematology
front: Outline the management of SCA.
back: Hydration and hydroxyurea (raises HbF); HSCT is the treatment of choice.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the Hb composition of sickle-cell trait and its protective effect.
back: HbA 60% + HbS 40%; protective against P. falciparum.

subject: Hematology
front: Codocytes are also known as what?

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of target cells (codocytes).
back: Thalassemia, IDA, liver disease, HbC, asplenia.

subject: Hematology
front: Dacrocytes are also known as what?

subject: Hematology
front: What causes teardrop cells (dacrocytes)?
back: Myelophthisis - myelofibrosis, metastasis, or granulomatous disease.

subject: Hematology
front: Schistocytes are also known as what?

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of schistocytes (helmet cells).
back: Macroangiopathic AHA (prosthetic heart valves) and MAHA (DIC, HUS, TTP).

subject: Hematology
front: Echinocytes are also known as what?

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of echinocytes (burr cells).
back: Renal failure, uremia, burns, PK deficiency, storage artefact, premature infants after blood transfusion.

subject: Hematology
front: Acanthocytes are also known as what?

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of acanthocytes (spur cells).
back: Severe liver disease, abetalipoproteinemia, vitamin E deficiency, hyposplenism.

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of rouleaux formation.
back: Multiple myeloma, autoimmune disease, early HIV, and diseases with raised ESR.

subject: Hematology
front: RBC agglutination on a smear is associated with what?

subject: Hematology
front: What hypersensitivity type and Coombs result characterize AIHA?
back: Type II hypersensitivity; direct Coombs positive.

subject: Hematology
front: Warm AIHA: temperature, causes, antigen/antibody, and hemolysis type?
back: 37 degrees; causes SLE/CLL/beta-lactams; Rh antigen with IgG antibody; both IVH and EVH.

subject: Hematology
front: What is Evan's syndrome?

subject: Hematology
front: Cold AIHA: temperature and causes?

subject: Hematology
front: Cold agglutinin: target antigen, antibody class, and hemolysis type?

subject: Hematology
front: Cold hemolysin: target antigen, antibody, and hemolysis type?
back: 'P' antigen; Donath-Landsteiner (IgG) antibody; intravascular, biphasic hemolysis.

subject: Hematology
front: What do cold agglutinin and cold hemolysin have in common regarding complement?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the triad of Plummer-Vinson (Paterson-Brown-Kelly) syndrome?
back: IDA, esophageal web, and atrophic glossitis.

subject: Hematology
front: Which organ produces hepcidin?

subject: Hematology
front: Hepcidin is regulated by which proteins, and what does dysfunction of each cause?
back: HFE and HJV (dysfunction -> hemochromatosis); TMPRSS6 (dysfunction -> iron-refractory IDA).

subject: Hematology
front: When hepcidin degrades ferroportin, what type of anemia results?

subject: Hematology
front: When hepcidin degrades CFU-E, what type of anemia results?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the gold-standard test for IDA?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the most sensitive test for IDA?

subject: Hematology
front: IDA: give serum iron, TIBC/transferrin, ferritin, and % transferrin saturation.
back: Serum iron down, TIBC/transferrin up, ferritin down, % saturation markedly down.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the earliest iron-study change in IDA?

subject: Hematology
front: ACD: give serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, and % transferrin saturation.
back: Serum iron down, TIBC down, ferritin up (due to inflammation), % saturation down.

subject: Hematology
front: Which cytokines are raised in anemia of chronic disease (ACD)?

subject: Hematology
front: Sideroblastic anemia: give serum iron, TIBC, ferritin, and % transferrin saturation.
back: Serum iron up, TIBC down, ferritin up, % saturation markedly up.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the management of sideroblastic anemia (iron overload)?

subject: Hematology
front: Which protoporphyrin marker is raised in IDA?

subject: Hematology
front: How many Fe molecules does one transferrin bind, and what is the normal % transferrin saturation?
back: Each transferrin binds 2 Fe (ideal = 6 Fe); % transferrin saturation = serum iron/TIBC = 2/6 x 100 = ~33%.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the Mentzer index formula and how it distinguishes IDA from thalassemia minor.
back: Mentzer index = MCV / RBC count; IDA > 13, thalassemia minor < 13.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the RDW values for IDA vs thalassemia minor.
back: IDA RDW > 14.5; thalassemia minor RDW 11.5 to 14.5.

subject: Hematology
front: List the three stages of IDA.
back: 1) Decreased iron storage; 2) iron-deficient erythropoiesis; 3) IDA (with peripheral-smear findings).

subject: Hematology
front: Pencil cells on the smear are seen in which anemia?

subject: Hematology
front: How is microcytosis defined on smear in IDA?
back: RBC smaller than a lymphocyte nucleus.

subject: Hematology
front: How is hypochromia defined on smear in IDA (and the normal RBC size)?
back: Central pallor greater than one-third of the cell; normal RBC = 7-8 micrometers.

subject: Hematology
front: Besides micro/hypochromia, list other peripheral-smear findings in IDA.
back: Poikilocytosis, anisocytosis (raised RDW), and reactive thrombocytosis.

subject: Hematology
front: List the clinical features of IDA.
back: Koilonychia, angular stomatitis, cheilitis, and pica.

subject: Hematology
front: In an iron-DEFICIENT state, what happens to iron uptake, transferrin-receptor synthesis, and transferrin-receptor mRNA?

subject: Hematology
front: In an iron-DEFICIENT state, what happens to iron storage and ferritin synthesis?

subject: Hematology
front: In an iron-EXCESS state, what happens to iron uptake, transferrin-receptor synthesis, and transferrin-receptor mRNA?

subject: Hematology
front: In an iron-EXCESS state, what happens to iron storage and ferritin synthesis?

subject: Hematology
front: What happens to ferritin mRNA concentration in iron-deficient vs iron-excess states?

subject: Hematology
front: Response to iron therapy at 12-24 hours?
back: Increased appetite, subjective improvement, decreased irritability, and replenishment of intracellular iron enzymes.

subject: Hematology
front: Response to iron therapy at 36-48 hours, and its significance?
back: Erythroid hyperplasia in the bone marrow - the earliest hematological response.

subject: Hematology
front: Response to iron therapy at 48-72 hours?

subject: Hematology
front: Response to iron therapy at 4-30 days?

subject: Hematology
front: Response to iron therapy at 1-3 months?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What reagent does the NESTROFT use, and how is a normal result read?
back: 0.3% NaCl; normally RBCs lyse so the solution clears and the line behind the tube is visible.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: In which conditions is osmotic fragility DECREASED?
back: Thalassemia and SCA (sickle cell anemia / thalassemia major).

deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: In which condition is osmotic fragility INCREASED (right-shifted curve)?

front: What is HPLC the gold standard for?

deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What medium and pH are used for Hb electrophoresis?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Give the Hb migration order from cathode to anode.
back: C, S, F, A (HbA migrates furthest toward the anode).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What does band 'A' represent on Hb electrophoresis?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Identify the conditions for bands AA, AF, AS, SS, AC, CC, SC.
back: AA = normal adult; AF = normal newborn; AS = sickle cell trait; SS = SCA; AC = HbC trait; CC = HbC disease; SC = HbS-C.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Sickle cell trait (AS) carries an increased risk of which cancer?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Which proteins are defective in PNH, and what is their normal role?
back: GPI-anchored proteins CD59, CD55 and C8-binding protein, which normally bind/inhibit complement.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Give the alternate names of CD59 and CD55.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What is the investigation of choice for PNH?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Name two other tests for PNH besides flow cytometry.
back: Ham's test (acidified serum lysis) and the sucrose lysis test.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Why is hemolysis worse at night in PNH?
back: Decreased pH at night (from CO2 retention) increases complement-mediated hemolysis.

deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What type of hemolysis occurs in PNH?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What is the most common cause of death in PNH?

deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What is the LAP score in PNH?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: PNH is associated with which marrow conditions?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What drug treats PNH and what does it target?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: At what level is the defect in PNH, and what is the curative treatment?
back: Defect is at the stem-cell level; TOC = HSCT.

subject: Hematology

front: What are the two broad classes of hematopoietic agents?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Name the EPO analogues and state which is longer-acting.
back: Epoetin alpha and darbepoietin (darbepoietin is longer-acting).

deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Name the EPO receptor ('R') agonist.

front: EPO is the drug of choice for anemia due to which causes?

back: G-CSF is more potent and less toxic.

back: Lenograstim and filgrastim (short-acting); long-acting derivatives are lipegfilgrastim and pegfilgrastim.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: Why must G-CSF be used cautiously in SCA?
back: It can trigger a sickle cell crisis (so it is contraindicated in SCA).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-4: Hemolytic Anaemia Tests & Hematopoietic Agents
front: What are the uses of G-CSF?
back: Neutropenia due to myelodysplasia, aplastic anemia, chemotherapy, and HIV.

subject: Hematology

back: Myeloblast; Auer rods are seen in AML.

subject: Hematology

back: Promyelocyte; it contains primary (1 degree) granules.

subject: Hematology
front: What granules appear at the myelocyte stage, and what do they determine?
back: Specific granules; the lineage (N/B/E - neutrophil/basophil/eosinophil) is decided at this stage.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the nucleus shapes of the myelocyte, metamyelocyte, and band form.
back: Myelocyte = 'D' shape; metamyelocyte = 'G' shape; band form = 'U' shape.

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of a leukemoid reaction.
back: Acute infection, stress, pregnancy, asplenia (SCA), malignancy, aplastic anemia, acute hemolysis/severe haemorrhage, DKA, Down's syndrome, MPN except CML, and hypophosphatemia.

subject: Hematology
front: What neutrophil changes does acute infection cause, and what are Dohle bodies?
back: Toxic granules and Dohle bodies (dilated ER); also seen in MDS.

subject: Hematology

back: CD10, CD19, CD20, CD21, CD23, CD79a.

back: Activated B cells, T cells, monocytes, and Reed-Sternberg cells.

subject: Hematology

subject: Hematology

back: DR, CD19, CD20, CD24, surface Ig.

back: TdT, CD2, CD3, CD4, CD5, CD7, CD8, CD34.

back: Vacuoles, E1 enzyme, X-linked, Autoinflammatory, Somatic.

subject: Hematology

back: It is a bridge between rheumatologic and hematologic disorders.

subject: Hematology
front: List the peripheral B-cell neoplasms.
back: DLBCL, Burkitt lymphoma, follicular lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma (splenic, nodal, extranodal), plasmacytoma/plasma cell myeloma, lymphoplasmacytic leukemia, CLL/SLL, B-cell prolymphocytic leukemia, hairy cell leukemia.

back: MDS with low blasts, MDS-hypoplastic, MDS with increased blasts (IB1 & IB2), MDS-fibrosis.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-8: WHO Classification of Hematolymphoid Tumors (2016)
front: List the childhood MDN (myelodysplastic neoplasm) subtypes.
back: Childhood MDS with low blasts; childhood MDS with high blasts.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-8: WHO Classification of Hematolymphoid Tumors (2016)
front: List the MDS/MPN entities.
back: CMML (most common), MDS/MPN with neutrophilia, MDS/MPN with SF3B1 mutation and thrombocytosis, MDS/MPN-NOS.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe FAB L1 ALL and its prognosis.
back: Small cells, scanty homogenous cytoplasm, round nucleus, no nucleoli; most common, best prognosis.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe FAB L2 ALL.
back: Large cells, moderate heterogenous cytoplasm, irregular nucleus, prominent nucleoli.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe FAB L3 ALL and its prognosis.
back: Large cells, abundant blue cytoplasm, round nucleus, prominent nucleoli; a.k.a. Burkitt-lymphoma-like; worst prognosis.

subject: Hematology
front: B-ALL: frequency, age, mutations, tissue/mediastinal involvement?
back: Most common; in children; LOF of PAX5/E2A/EBF; NO tissue or mediastinal involvement.

subject: Hematology
front: T-ALL: prognosis, age, mutation, tissue/mediastinum, special stain?
back: Worse prognosis; teenagers/adults; GOF of NOTCH1; tissue and mediastinal involvement; acid phosphatase positive.

subject: Hematology
front: List the good prognostic factors in ALL.
back: Age 2-9 yrs, white, female, B-ALL/CD10+/early pre-B cell, L1, TLC < 20x10^9/L, hyperploidy (MC), trisomy 4 & 7, t(12;21).

subject: Hematology
front: List the bad prognostic factors in ALL.
back: Age <1 or >10 yrs/adults, black, male, T-ALL, L2/L3, TLC > 50x10^9/L, hypoploidy/pseudodiploidy, BCR-ABL fusion (190 kD), t(4;11)/MLL-AF4 fusion.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the BCR-ABL fusion protein sizes in ALL, CML, and CNL.
back: ALL = 190 kD; CML = 210 kD; CNL = 230 kD.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the ALL lymphoblast.
back: Hand-mirror cells; scanty cytoplasm; 1 nucleolus; PAS positive (dot-positive, c.f. AML-M6).

subject: Hematology
front: List the risk factors for AML.
back: Radiation, P-vera, Kostmann syndrome (severe congenital neutropenia).

back: Acute promyelocytic leukemia; best prognosis; t(15;17) -> PML-RARA -> decreased Vit A -> decreased promyelocyte-to-myelocyte conversion; associated with DIC.

subject: Hematology

back: Acute myelomonocytic leukemia; inv(16); increased serum muramidase/lysozyme.

subject: Hematology
front: AML M5 (name, features)?
back: Acute monoblastic and monocytic leukemia; leukemia cutis and gum involvement.

subject: Hematology

back: Pure erythroid leukemia; a.k.a. Di Guglielmo disease; diffuse PAS positive (c.f. ALL).

subject: Hematology

back: Fever, neutrophilia, and edematous painful plaques (neutrophilic invasion).

back: Diagnosed with <20% blasts, except BCR-ABL1 and CEBPA which require >20% blasts.

back: MRD negative -> high-dose cytarabine; MRD positive -> HSCT.

subject: Hematology

subject: Hematology
front: What is the common pathophysiology of MPNs?
back: Activation of the growth-signalling pathway; panmyelosis.

subject: Hematology
front: What splenomegaly feature is seen in MPNs, and what is the exception?
back: Massive splenomegaly (dragging sensation in the abdomen) - except ET.

subject: Hematology
front: What are the natural-history risks of MPNs?
back: Increased risk of transformation to AML; the spent phase leads to myelofibrosis.

subject: Hematology
front: List the differential diagnoses of massive splenomegaly.
back: Malaria, kala-azar, Gaucher's, hairy cell leukemia, CLL, lymphoma, sarcoidosis, AIHA.

subject: Hematology
front: P-vera: RBC, WBC, platelets, JAK2?
back: RBC up-up, WBC up, platelets up, JAK2 +++. (P-vera is the most common MPN.)

subject: Hematology
front: ET: RBC, WBC, platelets, JAK2?
back: RBC normal, WBC normal, platelets up-up, JAK2 positive in 30-50% of cases.

subject: Hematology
front: PMF: RBC, WBC, platelets?
back: RBC down, WBC down, platelets down.

subject: Hematology
front: CML: RBC, WBC, platelets, JAK2?
back: RBC down, WBC up-up, platelets up, JAK2 negative.

front: Which JAK2-inhibitor drug can be used to manage MPNs?

subject: Hematology
front: Erythromelalgia occurs in which MPNs, and by what mechanism?
back: P-vera and ET; due to increased platelets causing vasoconstriction.

back: 1) Increased Hb (male 16.5/female 16) or increased Hct (male 49/female 48); 2) BM trilineage myeloproliferation with pleomorphic megakaryocytes; 3) JAK2 mutation positive.

back: Headache, TIA/stroke, MI, peptic ulcers, Budd-Chiari.

back: Dehydration, Gaisbock syndrome, dengue haemorrhagic fever.

front: List the causes of SECONDARY polycythemia (vs P-vera).
back: Cyanotic heart disease, smokers/COPD, high-altitude residents, EPO-secreting tumors.

subject: Hematology
front: How many criteria are required to diagnose ET?

subject: Hematology
front: List the diagnostic criteria of ET.
back: 1) Sustained platelet count > 450x10^9/L; 2) BM megakaryocytosis (enlarged mature) without increased WBC or RBC; 3) negative for P-vera/PMF/CML/MDS or any other myeloid neoplasm; 4) JAK2 V617F or MPL or calreticulin positive (or absence of those with no reactive thrombocytosis or IDA).

subject: Hematology
front: What are the consequences of increased platelets in ET?

subject: Hematology
front: Outline the management of ET.
back: No thrombosis risk -> observation; risk present -> low-dose aspirin + cytoreduction (hydroxyurea/busulfan/IFN-alpha).

subject: Hematology
front: What is the diagnostic threshold for PMF?

subject: Hematology
front: List the major diagnostic criteria of PMF.
back: 1) BM atypical megakaryocytosis with positive reticulin or collagen; 2) negative for P-vera/CML/MDS or other myeloid neoplasm; 3) JAK2 V617F or MPL or calreticulin positive (or absence of mutation -> rule out 2-degree fibrosis).

subject: Hematology
front: List the minor diagnostic criteria of PMF.
back: Leukoerythroblastosis, increased serum LDH, anemia, palpable splenomegaly, leukocytosis > 11x10^9/L.

subject: Hematology
front: What drives the fibrosis in PMF?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the nodal pattern of Hodgkin's lymphoma.
back: Single group of LNs, axial LNs, contiguous spread.

subject: Hematology
front: Which sites are NOT involved in HL (but are in NHL)?
back: Mesenteric nodes, Waldeyer's ring, and extranodal sites.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the age distribution of HL?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the nodal pattern of NHL.
back: Multiple, peripheral LNs, non-contiguous spread; mesenteric/Waldeyer/extranodal involved; elderly.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the IOC for Hodgkin's lymphoma?

subject: Hematology
front: What does the ABVD regimen for HL consist of?

subject: Hematology
front: What is the most common LN site in HL?

subject: Hematology
front: Give the markers of typical Reed-Sternberg cells.
back: CD15+, CD30+, EBV LMP+ (most specific marker = PAX5).

subject: Hematology
front: Give the chromosome and pathway of REL in HL.
back: REL on chromosome 2p -> NF-kB -> B-cell proliferation.

subject: Hematology
front: Lymphocyte predominant HL: cell, markers, prognosis?
back: Popcorn cell (lymphohistiocytic variant); EMA+, CD20+, CD45+, BCL6+, CD15-, CD30-, EBV LMP-; best prognosis.

subject: Hematology
front: Nodular sclerosis HL: cell, features, frequency, prognosis?
back: Lacunar cell; collagen nodules + mediastinal involvement + uncommon EBV; most common in the world; good prognosis; M=F.

subject: Hematology
front: Mixed cellularity HL: cell, features, frequency?
back: Classical RS (owl's eye); mixed background, max association with EBV/HIV/B-symptoms, bimodal age; most common in India.

subject: Hematology
front: Lymphocyte rich HL: cell?

subject: Hematology
front: Lymphocyte depleted HL: cell, host, prognosis?
back: Pleomorphic/mummified RS; immunocompromised host; worst prognosis.

subject: Hematology
front: What staging system is used for HL?

subject: Hematology
front: What do the suffixes A, B, and X mean in HL staging?
back: A = B symptoms absent; B = B symptoms present; X = >10 cm (bulky disease).

subject: Hematology
front: What is the IOC for HL staging?

subject: Hematology
front: Give Ann-Arbor stages I-IV.
back: I = single LN region; II = 2 or more regions same side of diaphragm; III = both sides of diaphragm (IIIs = spleen, IIIE = local/extranodal tissue); IV = disseminated extranodal involvement.

subject: Hematology
front: List the clinical features of HL.
back: Pel-Ebstein fever (waxing-waning), B symptoms, painless rubbery LNs.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the sex predilection of HL, and the exception?

subject: Hematology
front: List the paraneoplastic syndromes of HL.
back: Subacute cerebellar degeneration, minimal change disease, cholangitis, vanishing bile duct syndrome, pain on alcohol ingestion.

subject: Hematology
front: In HL prognosis, what matters more - stage or type?

subject: Hematology
front: List the poor prognostic factors in HL.
back: Age >45, Hb <10.5, WBC >15,000, serum albumin <4 g/dL, stage IV, absolute lymphocyte count <600/mm^3 (or <8% of leukocytes).

subject: Hematology
front: Chromosome 8 (c-myc): which lymphoma and which translocations?
back: Burkitt's lymphoma; t(8;14), t(8;22), t(2;8). (Note: t(8;21) is AML-M2, not Burkitt.)

subject: Hematology
front: t(14;18) is associated with which lymphoma?

subject: Hematology
front: List the components of the International Prognostic Index (IPI).
back: Age, serum LDH, Ann-Arbor stage, extranodal involvement, performance status.

subject: Hematology
front: NHL grade tends to be what in adults vs children?
back: Usually low grade in adults; high grade in children.

back: Initial CNS or bone marrow involvement.

subject: Hematology

subject: Hematology
front: List the key facts of DLBCL.
back: Diagnosis of exclusion; MC NHL; MC aggressive; MC extranodal; EBV+; can be bcl-2/myc/HAT+ (double/triple hit); poor prognosis; surface Ig+.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the LN histology of DLBCL.
back: Cells 4-5 times the size of a lymphocyte; sheets of pleomorphic lymphoid cells; complete effacement of LN architecture.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the variants of DLBCL.
back: Primary effusion lymphoma and immunodeficiency-associated DLBCL.

subject: Hematology
front: What markers and Ki67 characterize Burkitt lymphoma?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the LN histology of Burkitt lymphoma.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the clinical types of Burkitt lymphoma.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the behavior and key complication of Burkitt lymphoma.
back: Aggressive but chemosensitive; most common cause of tumor lysis syndrome.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the BM finding in Burkitt lymphoma?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe follicular lymphoma (CD10+/BCL2+ branch).
back: Best prognosis; centrocytes + centroblasts; can transform into DLBCL.

subject: Hematology
front: The CD5+ branch splits into which two lymphomas?

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: markers and deletions?

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: smear appearance and characteristic cells?
back: Convent-girl appearance; smudge/basket/parachute cells (due to loss of vimentin).

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: pathophysiology via BTK?
back: BTK mutation -> increased BTK -> increased B cells -> increased IgGs -> AIHA and thrombocytopenia.

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: diagnostic criteria?

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: pathognomonic LN finding?
back: Proliferation centers (small lymphoid cells + activated lymphoid cells).

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: good prognostic markers?

subject: Hematology
front: CLL/SLL: poor prognostic markers?
back: Deletion 11q/17p, NOTCH1 mutation, ZAP-70 positive, lack of somatic hypermutation.

subject: Hematology
front: Mantle cell lymphoma: markers and cells?
back: CD5+, CD23-, Cyclin D1+, SOX11+; cleaved/buttock cells; lymphomatoid polyposis; poor prognosis.

subject: Hematology
front: Marginal zone lymphoma: markers?

subject: Hematology
front: Marginal zone lymphoma: inflammatory associations and MC site?
back: Sjogren's, H. pylori, Hashimoto's; MC site = stomach.

subject: Hematology
front: What marker identifies T-cell lymphomas?

subject: Hematology
front: Cutaneous T-cell lymphoma: cell and origin?
back: Sezary cell (cerebriform nuclei); arises from mycosis fungoides.

subject: Hematology
front: Adult T-cell leukemia: cell, virus, gene?

subject: Hematology
front: Anaplastic large cell lymphoma: cell and gene?

subject: Hematology
front: Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma: key features?
back: Destructive nasopharyngeal mass; associated with EBV; highly aggressive; radiosensitive but chemoresistant.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: What two requirements must be met to diagnose MM?
back: 1) Clonal BM plasma cells >=10% OR biopsy-proven bony/extramedullary plasmacytoma; AND 2) CRAB feature or a defining (SLiM) biomarker.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: CRAB - what does C stand for?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: CRAB - what does R stand for?
back: Renal insufficiency (creatinine >2 mg/dL or creatinine clearance <40 mL/min).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: CRAB - what does A stand for?
back: Anemia (Hb <10 g/dL or 2 g/dL below normal).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: CRAB - what does B stand for, and the MC site?
back: Bone lesions (>=1 osteolytic lesion on skeletal X-ray/whole-body MRI/whole-body FDG PET-CT); MC site = vertebrae.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MM defining biomarkers (SLiM) - what is S?
back: Greater than or equal to 60% clonal plasma cells in the BM.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: SLiM - what is Li?
back: Involved/uninvolved free light chain ratio >=100 (involved FLC >=100 mg/L).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: SLiM - what is M?
back: MRI with more than one focal marrow lesion.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MM skull X-ray finding?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MM peripheral smear finding (and why)?
back: Rouleaux (due to increased Ig and fibrinogen).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MM serum electrophoresis finding?
back: M-spike (M-protein) in the gamma region, >3 g/dL.

subject: Hematology

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Describe the MM plasma cell (features not seen on PS).
back: Oval with eccentric nucleus, clockface/cartwheel chromatin, perinuclear hof/halo (Golgi zone).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: What are Mulberry/Mott cells?
back: Plasma cells with Igs forming grape-like clusters.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: What are flame cells?
back: Plasma cells with Igs appearing like a flame.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Russell body - location?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Dutcher body - location (and other association)?

subject: Hematology

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MM good-prognosis cytogenetics?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Give the MM cytokine pathway.
back: Increased IL-6 -> increased plasma cells -> Igs -> positive MiP1-alpha.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: What is the effect of MiP1-alpha on bone?
back: Increased osteoclast activity and decreased osteoblast activity.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Outline the management of MM.
back: Chemotherapy (lenalidomide + bortezomib + dexamethasone) followed by autologous HSCT; if ineligible, continue chemotherapy.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Monoclonal complications of MM?
back: AL amyloidosis (-> pinch purpura) and Bence-Jones proteinuria.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Give the MM immunoglobulin frequency order.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Which MM Ig types cause hyperviscosity?

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Features of IgD myeloma?
back: Renal failure (decreased EPO), poor prognosis, no M-spike.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Describe the behavior of Bence-Jones proteins.
back: Precipitate at 40-60 C, disappear on boiling, and reappear on cooling (normal proteins precipitate at 100 C).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: MGUS (most common) criteria?
back: BM <10%, serum M-protein <3 g/dL, NO CRAB or SLIM.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Smoldering myeloma (asymptomatic) criteria?
back: BM >10%, serum M-protein >3 g/dL, but NO CRAB.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Plasma cell leukemia criterion?
back: More than 20% plasma cells on peripheral smear.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Features of Waldenstrom's macroglobulinemia?
back: Increased IgM -> hyperviscosity syndrome; MYD88 mutation; associated with lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma; no decreased Ca2+/lytic lesions/plasmacytomas.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-14: Multiple Myeloma & Plasma Cell Disorders
front: Name two other plasma cell disorders (eponymous syndromes).

subject: Hematology
front: List the contents of platelet alpha-granules.
back: P-selectin, PDGF, PF4, TGF-beta, fibrinogen, factor 5, vWF.

subject: Hematology
front: Which disorders result from alpha-granule deficiency?
back: Grey platelet syndrome and Quebec platelet syndrome.

subject: Hematology
front: List the contents of platelet dense (delta) granules.

subject: Hematology
front: Which disorder results from dense-granule deficiency?

subject: Hematology
front: Step 1 (Injury): what happens?
back: Endothelial damage -> transient vasoconstriction via the neural stimulation reflex and endothelin (released from damaged cells).

subject: Hematology
front: Step 2 (Exposure): what binds, and where does vWF come from?
back: vWF binds exposed collagen; vWF comes from Weibel-Palade bodies of endothelial cells and alpha-granules of platelets.

subject: Hematology
front: Step 3 (Adhesion): how do platelets bind?
back: Platelets bind vWF via the GpIb receptor at the site of injury, then undergo a conformational change.

subject: Hematology
front: During adhesion, what do platelets release?
back: ADP and Ca2+ (necessary for the coagulation cascade) and TXA2.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe the platelet shape change on activation.
back: Round/disc-like -> sea-urchin-like (spikes/protrusions) with increased surface area.

subject: Hematology
front: What creates the negative charge that activates the coagulation cascade?
back: Phosphatidylserine moving to the outer surface.

subject: Hematology
front: Step 4 (Activation): describe the pathway.
back: ADP binds the P2Y12 receptor -> induces GpIIb/IIIa expression -> rapid irreversible aggregation.

subject: Hematology
front: Step 5 (Aggregation): what links platelets?
back: Fibrinogen binds GpIIb/IIIa receptors and links platelets.

subject: Hematology
front: List the pro-aggregation factors.
back: TXA2 (from platelets) and decreased blood flow.

subject: Hematology
front: List the anti-aggregation factors.
back: PGI2 and NO (from endothelial cells) and increased blood flow.

subject: Hematology
front: GpIb deficiency causes which disorder?

subject: Hematology
front: GpIIb/IIIa deficiency causes which disorder?

subject: Hematology
front: Give the CD numbers of GpIIb and GpIIIa.

subject: Hematology
front: Antibodies against GpIIb/IIIa cause what?

front: vWF deficiency causes which disorder?

subject: Hematology
front: What do abciximab/eptifibatide/tirofiban target?

front: What are the contents of a Weibel-Palade body?

subject: Hematology
front: Describe a platelet-type bleeding presentation.
back: Petechiae, purpura, increased bleeding time (normal BT 2-9 min).

subject: Hematology
front: Which drugs cause drug-induced thrombocytopenia?

subject: Hematology
front: Normal platelet count + abnormal ristocetin aggregation indicates?

subject: Hematology
front: Normal platelet count + abnormal ADP/collagen aggregation indicates?

subject: Hematology
front: Reduced platelets + immature platelet >6% indicates?

subject: Hematology
front: Reduced platelets + immature platelet <6% indicates?

subject: Hematology
front: ITP: Coombs result and hypersensitivity type?

subject: Hematology
front: Features of acute ITP?
back: <6 months, without preceding viral infection, in children, self-limiting, TPC markedly decreased, F=M.

subject: Hematology
front: Features of chronic ITP?
back: More than 6 months, in adolescents/adults, TPC decreased, F:M = 3:1.

subject: Hematology
front: Management of ITP?
back: Steroids, IVIG, immunosuppressants, eltrombopag, romiplostim, fostamatinib.

subject: Hematology
front: ITP and the spleen?
back: No splenomegaly (but splenectomy resolves the thrombocytopenia).

subject: Hematology
front: Causes of secondary ITP?
back: HIV, Hep C, SLE, CLL, H. pylori.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the mechanism of TTP/HUS?
back: ADAMTS13 mutation -> accumulation of HMW vWF multimers -> platelet activation -> thrombosis.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the congenital form of TTP called?

subject: Hematology
front: Give the pentad of TTP.
back: MAHA, thrombocytopenia, renal failure, neurological abnormality, fever.

subject: Hematology
front: What causes typical HUS?

subject: Hematology
front: What causes atypical HUS?
back: Complement defect (Factor H, I) or CD46 defect.

subject: Hematology
front: Give the triad of HUS.

subject: Hematology
front: Describe a coagulation-type bleeding presentation and the normal PT/aPTT.
back: Ecchymosis, hemarthrosis, increased clotting time; normal PT 11-15s, normal aPTT 30-35s.

subject: Hematology
front: PT raised (aPTT normal): causes and why?
back: Factor VII deficiency, early Vit K deficiency, early warfarin - because VII has the shortest half-life among factors 2, 7, 9, 10.

subject: Hematology
front: aPTT raised (PT normal) in an adult: causes?
back: APLA and acquired hemophilia (factor inhibitor, h/o repeated transfusions; detected by the Bethesda assay).

subject: Hematology
front: aPTT raised in a child: causes by factor and sex?
back: Male -> Hemophilia A (VIII, XLR) and Hemophilia B/Christmas disease (IX, XLR); Female -> Hemophilia C (XI, AR).

subject: Hematology
front: Parahaemophilia is a deficiency of which factor?

subject: Hematology
front: Both PT and aPTT normal: which deficiency?

subject: Hematology
front: Why can hemophilia manifest in females?

subject: Hematology
front: Both PT and aPTT raised: causes?
back: DIC, factor X deficiency, afibrinogenemia, late Vit K deficiency.

subject: Hematology
front: Hemophilia A, mild (VIII 6-50% of normal): management?

subject: Hematology
front: Hemophilia A, severe (VIII <1%): management?
back: Recombinant VIII concentrate / cryoprecipitate (FFP is deficient in VIII); 1 unit raises VIII by 2%.

subject: Hematology
front: Hemophilia B: management?
back: Recombinant factor / FFP (cryoprecipitate is deficient in IX); 1 unit raises IX by 1%.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the alternate name for DIC?

subject: Hematology
front: List the lab findings in DIC.
back: Decreased TPC; increased BT, CT, PT, aPTT; schistocytes on PS; increased FDP; increased D-dimer (most specific).

subject: Hematology
front: List the causes of DIC.
back: Septic abortion, eclampsia, severe toxemia, burns, APML, colon/rectum/pancreas cancers.

subject: Hematology
front: What is Kasabach-Merritt syndrome?

subject: Hematology
front: Name clotting factors I-V.
back: I = Fibrinogen; II = Prothrombin; III = Tissue thromboplastin; IV = Calcium ions; V = Labile factor/Proaccelerin.

subject: Hematology
front: Name clotting factors VII-XIII.
back: VII = Stable factor/Proconvertin; VIII = Antihemophilic factor A; IX = Christmas factor/PTC; X = Stuart-Prower factor; XI = Plasma thromboplastin antecedent (PTA); XII = Hageman factor; XIII = Fibrin-stabilising factor/Laki-Lorand factor.

subject: Hematology
front: Where are clotting factors produced, and what are the exceptions?
back: All in the liver except VIII (endothelial cells) and XIII (bone marrow).

subject: Hematology
front: The intrinsic pathway is measured by which test, and what activates it for testing?
back: aPTT; activated with kaolin for testing.

subject: Hematology
front: The extrinsic pathway is measured by which test, and what activates it for testing?
back: PT; activated with Ca2+/brain extract (tissue factor) for testing.

subject: Hematology
front: What does the kinin cascade produce, and what are bradykinin's effects?
back: Kallikrein -> bradykinin -> increased vasodilation, permeability, and pain.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the regulatory anticoagulant proteins.

subject: Hematology
front: Which factors do Proteins C and S inhibit?

subject: Hematology
front: What activates Protein C, and where is it found?
back: Thrombomodulin (found throughout the circulation except cerebral).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the anticoagulants shown in the cascade.
back: Heparin, LMWH, and direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban, bivalirudin, dabigatran).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the thrombolytics.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the antifibrinolytics.

subject: Hematology
front: What does tPA convert?

subject: Hematology
front: What does plasmin produce?

subject: Hematology
front: Name the regulators of fibrinolysis/anticoagulation.

subject: Hematology
front: Protein C deficiency increases the risk of what?
back: DVT, purpura fulminans (severe DIC), and warfarin-induced skin necrosis.

subject: Hematology
front: vWD: alternate name and epidemiological significance?
back: Pseudohemophilia; it is the most common inherited bleeding disorder.

subject: Hematology
front: List the sources of vWF.

subject: Hematology
front: vWF function 1 (adhesion) and the resulting defect?
back: Platelet adhesion via GpIb -> a bleeding defect (increased BT, normal TPC).

subject: Hematology
front: vWF function 2 (carrier of VIII) and the resulting defect?
back: Carrier of factor VIII (raises its half-life from 2.4h to 12h) -> a clotting defect (increased CT, increased aPTT, normal PT, decreased VIII assay).

subject: Hematology
front: vWD: RIPA and ReCoF findings?
back: Per the notes, RIPA (ristocetin-induced aggregation) is marked increased and ReCoF (ristocetin cofactor assay) is decreased. (FLAG: standard teaching usually shows decreased RIPA except in type 2B.)

subject: Hematology
front: vWD Type 1: inheritance, defect, frequency, and Mx?
back: AD; partial deficiency (most common); treated with desmopressin.

subject: Hematology
front: vWD Type 3: inheritance, defect, and Mx?
back: AR; complete absence; treated with Factor VIII infusions.

subject: Hematology
front: vWD Type 2 represents what kind of defect, and list its subtypes.
back: Qualitative defects; 2A (AD) adhesion defect, 2B (AD) thrombocytopenia, 2M (AD) adhesion defect, 2N (AR) called autosomal hemophilia.

subject: Hematology
front: Osler-Weber-Rendu: alternate name, inheritance, and defect?
back: Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia; autosomal dominant; defective TGF-beta signalling.

subject: Hematology
front: List the Curacao criteria for HHT.
back: Epistaxis (spontaneous recurrent nosebleeds), family history (first-degree relative with HHT), telangiectasias (lips/oral cavity/fingers/nose), visceral lesions (GI telangiectasia, pulmonary/hepatic/cerebral/spinal AVM).

subject: Hematology
front: Give the diagnostic thresholds for HHT.
back: Definite if 3 criteria present; possible/suspected if 2; unlikely if fewer than 2.

subject: Hematology
front: Which antiplatelet drugs are 'hit and run' (act longer than their half-life due to irreversible binding)?
back: Aspirin, irreversible P2Y12 receptor blockers, ADP inhibitors, and PPIs.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the classes of antiplatelet drugs.
back: Cox inhibitors, P2Y12 receptor blockers, GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors, phosphodiesterase inhibitors, and protease-activated receptor (PAR) inhibitor.

subject: Hematology
front: Aspirin: class and dose?

subject: Hematology
front: List the uses of aspirin.
back: 2-degree prophylaxis of ACS (UA, MI) and ischemic stroke; treatment of ACS, Kawasaki disease, essential thrombocythemia, APLA.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the main side effect of aspirin?

subject: Hematology
front: Name the irreversible P2Y12 blockers.

subject: Hematology
front: Ticlopidine: side effects (why is it not used)?
back: Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, agranulocytosis, TTP-HUS (so it is not used).

subject: Hematology
front: Clopidogrel: activation and key interaction?
back: Activated by CYP2C19 (so contraindicated with omeprazole); used for 2-degree prophylaxis of ACS/stroke and treatment of ACS.

subject: Hematology
front: Prasugrel: properties and contraindication?
back: Most potent, fastest-acting; used for PCI in STEMI; contraindicated in stroke/TIA (increased risk of IC bleed).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the reversible P2Y12 blockers.

subject: Hematology
front: Ticagrelor: properties and use?
back: Non-competitive, long-acting, oral; 2-degree prophylaxis of ACS and stroke.

subject: Hematology
front: Cangrelor: properties and use?
back: Competitive, short-acting, IV; PCI in STEMI.

subject: Hematology
front: GP IIb/IIIa monoclonal antibody: name and properties?
back: Abciximab (also inhibits vitronectin); shortest half-life but longest-acting (max affinity); IV/intracoronary.

subject: Hematology
front: GP IIb/IIIa synthetic molecules: names and properties?
back: Eptifibatide and tirofiban; longest half-life but shortest-acting (min affinity).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the phosphodiesterase inhibitors and their use.
back: Dipyridamole and cilostazol; used as vasodilators.

subject: Hematology
front: PAR inhibitor: name, use, and contraindication?
back: Vorapaxar; 2-degree prophylaxis of ACS; contraindicated in stroke/TIA (increased risk of IC hemorrhage).

subject: Hematology
front: Parenteral indirect anticoagulants: mechanism?
back: Activate AT-III; bind to and inhibit factors IIa and Xa.

subject: Hematology
front: UFH: monitoring?

subject: Hematology
front: List the uses of UFH.
back: STEMI/UA, cardioembolic stroke, pulmonary embolism, catheter-induced thrombosis, cancer-induced thrombosis, patient on anticoagulant requiring thrombolysis.

subject: Hematology
front: LMWH: examples and action ratio?

subject: Hematology
front: LMWH/Fondaparinux: contraindication and HIT note?
back: Contraindicated in renal failure (except UFH); does not cause HIT.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the antidote for heparin?

subject: Hematology
front: List the side effects of heparin.

subject: Hematology
front: List the contraindications of heparin.
back: Thrombocytopenia, endocarditis, alcoholics, cirrhosis, severe HTN, eye surgery, neuro surgery, renal failure (except UFH).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the parenteral direct thrombin inhibitors.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the parenteral direct factor Xa inhibitor.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the DOC for HIT, and its renal caveat?
back: Direct thrombin inhibitors (argatroban is DOC); contraindicated in renal failure except argatroban (hepatic excretion).

subject: Hematology
front: HIT Type 1: timing, mechanism, course?
back: <5 days after treatment; due to platelet aggregation; self-limiting (most common).

subject: Hematology
front: HIT Type 2: timing, mechanism, consequence?
back: 5-14 days after treatment; due to PF4 antibodies -> arterial and venous thrombosis; needs treatment.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the IOC for HIT?

subject: Hematology
front: Outline the management of HIT.
back: Argatroban (DOC), shift to fondaparinux (4 weeks to 3 months); when TPC >150,000 start warfarin or a DOAC (warfarin is C/I in active HIT, given only after resolution).

subject: Hematology
front: Name the oral indirect anticoagulant.

subject: Hematology
front: What are the reversal agents for warfarin?

subject: Hematology
front: Warfarin target INR (general, surgery, mechanical valves)?
back: 2-3 (1.4 in case of surgery; mechanical valves 2.5-3.5).

subject: Hematology
front: Warfarin high-INR management?
back: INR >3 but <10 -> stop, restart after INR normal; >10 & asymptomatic -> stop + oral Vit K; symptomatic -> stop + PTC (>FFP) + IV Vit K.

subject: Hematology
front: Warfarin DOC indications?
back: Prophylaxis of thrombosis in A.fib with mechanical valve/renal failure/severe MS/patient on P-glycoprotein; APLA; splanchnic vein thrombosis.

subject: Hematology
front: List the side effects of warfarin.
back: Bleeding, skin necrosis (first 5 days), alopecia, warfarin embryopathy, bluish discoloration of feet.

subject: Hematology
front: Oral direct anticoagulants (DOACs): DOC indication?
back: Prophylaxis of thrombosis in non-valvular A.fib.

subject: Hematology
front: Oral direct thrombin inhibitor and its antidote?

subject: Hematology
front: Oral direct factor Xa inhibitors and their antidote?
back: Apixaban, edoxaban, rivaroxaban; antidote = andexanet alpha.

subject: Hematology
front: What is the universal antidote for all direct anticoagulants?

subject: Hematology
front: Streptokinase: mechanism and drawbacks?
back: Binds plasminogen and exposes the tPA binding site; breaks fibrin in both clot and plasma -> increased bleeding risk; high dose required; neutralised by anti-streptococcal antibodies.

subject: Hematology
front: Recombinant tPA agents and their key property?

subject: Hematology
front: List the uses of fibrinolytics.
back: STEMI, massive pulmonary embolism, peripheral thrombosis (direct site injection).

subject: Hematology
front: List the contraindications of fibrinolytics.
back: NSTEMI/UA, brain tumor/aneurysm, recent surgery/trauma, aortic dissection, h/o IC haemorrhage.

subject: Hematology
front: Name the antifibrinolytics.

subject: Hematology
front: List the uses of antifibrinolytics.
back: Thrombolytic-induced bleeding, preventing procedural bleeding in hemophiliacs, upper GI/surgical/trauma bleed, menorrhagia.

subject: Hematology
front: List the contraindications of antifibrinolytics.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Windkessel vessels: examples and function?
back: Aorta and large arteries (conductance vessels); maintain BP via elastic recoil (Windkessel effect) from abundant elastic fibres.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Resistance vessels: which vessels and features?
back: Arterioles; maximum wall thickness-to-lumen ratio; regulate blood flow (along with pre-capillary sphincters).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Exchange vessels: which vessels and features?
back: Capillaries (microcirculation); about 5% of blood volume; no smooth muscle; single layer of endothelium with BM; continuous/fenestrated/sinusoidal types.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Capacitance vessels: which vessels and key feature?
back: Venules and veins; they hold the maximum blood volume (~54%).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Shunt vessels: which vessels and features?
back: AV anastomoses; capillaries with no exchange; thick muscular walls with rich innervation; in skin/fingertips/ear lobes; for temperature regulation.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: What is vasomotion, and how is it regulated?
back: Intermittent contraction of arterioles and pre-capillary sphincters causing intermittent blood flow; mainly regulated by tissue O2.

deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: Name the three tunics of an artery.
back: Tunica intima, tunica media, tunica externa.

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: What is the distinguishing feature of an elastic artery?
back: Internal + external elastic laminae (very high elastin).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: What is the distinguishing feature of an arteriole?
back: Very high smooth muscle (in the tunica media).

subject: Hematology
deck: Part-18: Blood Vessels & Blood Cell Counting
front: State Thoma's L rule.
back: Cells touching the L-shaped (left and bottom) lines are NOT counted; cells touching the top-and-right lines ARE counted.

back: Vol of 1 large box = 1x1x1/10 = 1/10 mm^3; both sides counted -> 2 x 1/10 = 2/10 mm^3.
